title: The field of a charged rod: where algebra runs out
scenario: A rod of length $L = 0.3$ m with linear charge density $\lambda = 100$ nC/m lies along the axis, its near end $a = 0.1$ m from the point where we want the field. Algebra gives the field of a *point* charge, $kq/r^2$ — but a rod is not a point, and there is no algebra formula for charge spread along a length. Calculus is the only way in: slice the rod into point charges $dq = \lambda\,dx$, each contributing $k\,dq/x^2$, and integrate. The shaded area under those contributions is the field $E = k\lambda(1/a - 1/(a+L))$, which collapses to the point-charge $kQ/a^2$ when the rod is short.

assumptions.claim: A thin rod with uniform linear charge density $\lambda$, and the field evaluated at a point on the rod's axis beyond its near end (so every slice lies along the same line and the contributions add as scalars).

steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (and where it stops)
steps.prose: Algebra gives the field of a single point charge. But a charged rod is not a point — the charge is spread along its length, every piece a different distance away — and there is no algebra formula for that. This is where algebra runs out.

near_half_field.label: Field from the near half of the rod

steps.label: Slice the rod into point charges and add their fields
steps.prose: Cut the rod into slices of length $dx$, each carrying charge $dq = \lambda\,dx$ and acting like a point charge at distance $x$. Its field is $k\,dq/x^2$. Summing over the rod is an integral — the only way to handle a continuous distribution.

steps.label: The total field is the area under the contributions
steps.prose: Integrating the inverse-square contributions gives a finite field — the shaded area under $k\lambda/x^2$. The slope of the accumulated-field curve is the contribution at the cursor; the area under the contributions is the field.

steps.label: It reduces to the point charge when the rod is short
steps.prose: Writing $Q = \lambda L$, the rod's field is $kQ/(a(a+L))$. When the rod is much shorter than its distance, this becomes $kQ/a^2$ — the point-charge formula, recovered. Drag the cursor to sweep how much of the rod is included, and $\lambda$ to scale the charge.

proof.heading: There is no algebra formula for a continuous charge — calculus sums the point-charge contributions, and the area under them is the field.
proof.detail: check $dE/dx = k\lambda/x^2$; check $E = \int_a^x k\lambda/x'^2\,dx'$; total $kQ/(a(a+L))$; short-rod limit $kQ/a^2$

checks.claim: The accumulated field's slope is the contribution density: $\dfrac{dE}{dx} = \dfrac{k\lambda}{x^2}$.
The field is the area under the contributions: $E(x) = \int_a^x \dfrac{k\lambda}{x'^2}\,dx'$.
The whole rod gives $E = k\lambda\left(\dfrac1a - \dfrac1{a+L}\right) = \dfrac{kQ}{a(a+L)}$ with $Q = \lambda L$.
For a short rod the field reduces to a point charge: $E/Q \to k/a^2$ as $L \to 0$.

misconception.claim: To get the rod's field, just treat all its charge as sitting at the rod's centre (or near end) and use $kQ/r^2$.
misconception.refuted_by: Lumping the charge at one point gives the wrong answer, because the field is non-linear in distance: the near slices ($x$ small) contribute far more than the far ones ($k/x^2$ falls off steeply), so you cannot replace the spread-out charge with a single point at the average position. Treating the rod as a point at its near end *overestimates* the field; the true field is the integral $E = k\lambda(1/a - 1/(a+L)) = kQ/(a(a+L))$, which is genuinely smaller. Only when the rod is much shorter than its distance ($L \ll a$) do the slices all sit at nearly the same distance and the integral collapses back to the point charge $kQ/a^2$. The cursor sweeps how much of the rod is included; the area under the contribution curve is the field.

practice.asks: the field from the charged rod
practice.prompt: A rod of length $L = 0.3$ m carries linear charge density $\lambda = 100$ nC/m. What is the electric field at a point $a = 0.1$ m beyond one end, on the axis?

choices.misconception: Treated the whole rod as a point charge at the near distance $a$: $kQ/a^2$. That over-counts the far part; integrating $\int k\,dq/x^2$ along the rod gives the smaller $k\lambda L/[a(a+L)]$.
Put all the charge at the far end ($x = a+L$). The true field lies between the near-end and far-end point-charge estimates.

algebra_steps.label: What the algebra-based course hands you (and where it stops)
algebra_steps.prose: Algebra gives the field of a single point charge. But a charged rod is not a point — the charge is spread along its length, every piece a different distance away — and there is no algebra formula for that. This is where algebra runs out.

calculus_steps.label: Slice the rod into point charges and add their fields
calculus_steps.prose: Cut the rod into slices of length $dx$, each carrying charge $dq = \lambda\,dx$ and acting like a point charge at distance $x$. Its field is $k\,dq/x^2$. Summing over the rod is an integral — the only way to handle a continuous distribution.

calculus_steps.label: The total field is the area under the contributions
calculus_steps.prose: Integrating the inverse-square contributions gives a finite field — the shaded area under $k\lambda/x^2$. The slope of the accumulated-field curve is the contribution at the cursor; the area under the contributions is the field.

calculus_steps.label: It reduces to the point charge when the rod is short
calculus_steps.prose: Writing $Q = \lambda L$, the rod's field is $kQ/(a(a+L))$. When the rod is much shorter than its distance, this becomes $kQ/a^2$ — the point-charge formula, recovered. Drag the cursor to sweep how much of the rod is included, and $\lambda$ to scale the charge.
